name: Subarctic Seasonal Round
description: The seasonal cycle of the boreal forest and subarctic transition zone — the world of the Cree, Innu, Attikamek, and Dene. Defined by caribou migration, freeze-up and break-up, and the demands of survival in a land of long winters and brief, intense summers.

seasons.name: Goose Break / Spring Hunt
ecologicalMarkers: Canada geese and snow geese fly north in massive flocks
River and lake ice begins to break up ('les glaces')
Ptarmigan change from winter white to mottled brown
Caribou begin moving north to calving grounds
activities: Goose hunting — a major spring harvest for Cree communities (Eeyou/Eeyouch)
Goose camp setup — families move to traditional goose-hunting grounds
Goose plucking, smoking, and preserving
Watching for caribou movement northward
Repairing and launching birchbark canoes after winter storage
Setting beaver traps as beaver become active at break-up
seasons.culturalContext: Spring goose break is one of the most important cultural events in Eeyou (Cree of northern Quebec) life. Families leave permanent communities to travel to traditional goose-hunting territories, maintaining multi-generational connections to specific hunting grounds. This is a Level 1 heritage skill area — goose camp practices are publicly shared by Eeyou communities.

seasons.name: Open Water / Fishing Season
ecologicalMarkers: Lakes and rivers fully open
Black flies at peak (insects define subarctic summer)
Caribou calves born on the tundra/barren grounds transition
Brook trout and pike active near surface
activities: Intensive fishing — whitefish, Arctic char, lake trout, pike, walleye depending on region
Fish smoking and drying for winter storage — large quantities processed
Birchbark harvesting for canoe repair and container construction
Gathering berries as they ripen — cloudberries (bakeapple), blueberries, Labrador tea
Moose hunting and hide preparation begins
Canoe travel along river systems — summer travel mode
seasons.culturalContext: Summer is the most active season for travel, fishing, and food production. The window is short — even in July, frost can occur at night in the deep subarctic. Fish smoking and drying during this period provided a critical portion of the winter food supply.

seasons.name: Berry Moon / Caribou Season
ecologicalMarkers: Caribou begin moving south from barren grounds (Chipewyan, Innu)
Wild berries — Labrador tea, cloudberries, crowberries — reach peak
First frost arrives
Lakes and rivers begin to cool — fishing shifts to deep water
activities: Caribou hunting as herds move through
Caribou hide preparation — scraping, stretching, brain-tanning
Pemmican preparation — mixing dried lean caribou meat, rendered fat, dried berries
Berry picking and preservation — dried, used in pemmican
Collecting spruce gum for canoe repair while still traveling
Setting up fall camps at traditional locations
seasons.culturalContext: The fall caribou hunt was the single most important food-getting event of the year for many subarctic peoples. A successful hunt could provide the majority of a family's winter protein and fat, plus hide for clothing and shelter. Pemmican making — combining dried meat, rendered fat, and berries — was a critical preservation technique.

seasons.name: Freeze-Up / Trapping Season
ecologicalMarkers: Ponds and small lakes freeze first
River systems freeze progressively from edges
Snow arrives and deepens
Moose enter rut and are more visible
activities: Setting trap lines for beaver, muskrat, marten, mink, otter, lynx
Moose hunting — fall rut makes bulls vulnerable to calling
Moose hide preparation
Snowshoe construction and repair — essential for all winter movement
Building and preparing winter shelter — canvas or hide tent with stove for trappers
Toboggan construction and preparation for winter hauling
seasons.culturalContext: Freeze-up marks the transition from water travel to land and snow travel. The ability to read ice safety and navigate by snowshoe became survival skills. Trap line management required extensive knowledge of animal behavior and the subarctic ecosystem.

seasons.name: Deep Winter
ecologicalMarkers: Temperatures can reach -40°C/-40°F or colder
Snow depth at maximum — 1-2 meters in boreal forest
Ptarmigan congregate in spruce groves
Moose yard up in dense spruce cover
activities: Running and checking trap lines by snowshoe and toboggan
Ice fishing on frozen lakes — jigging for whitefish and lake trout
Ptarmigan snaring
Hide tanning and sewing in camp
Moccasin and snowshoe webbing repair
Storytelling and teaching — winter camp evenings
Firewood management — keeping the fire is survival
seasons.culturalContext: Deep winter in the subarctic is the most demanding season. Skill in the cold — reading weather, managing fire, maintaining equipment, navigating in whiteout conditions — was the difference between survival and death. Heritage skills in this context are not leisurely hobbies; they are the technology of survival.

seasons.name: Late Winter / Hunger Moon
ecologicalMarkers: Days lengthening noticeably
Cached food supplies running low ('hunger moon')
Moose become accessible on crusted snow (moose sink, hunters on snowshoes do not)
First signs of spring migration birds
activities: Intensive moose hunting on crusted snow — critical period for food resupply
Continuing trap line work
Snowshoe travel at maximum efficiency on hardened crust
Repairing equipment in anticipation of spring travel
Setting snares for snowshoe hares at population peak years
seasons.culturalContext: The period of late winter before geese returned was historically the most difficult — the 'hunger months.' Heritage skills at this time were about knowing where to find food when conventional supply was exhausted. The ability to hunt moose on snowshoes in crusted-snow conditions was a critical survival skill.
